ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Bi Cuon (Vietnamese pork skin roll). Closest defensible anchor is pork skin food product (FOODON_03315595). Dish-level composite entry does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pig skin, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No composite dish-level code for Bi Cuon exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Bi Cuon or pork skin roll dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular dish-level food codes for Southeast Asian preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bi Cuon is one roll weighing approximately 80–100 g, consisting of rice paper wrapped around shredded pork skin, roasted rice powder, and fresh herbs, often served with a fish sauce-based dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Skin Roll (Bi Cuon) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Skin Roll (Bi Cuon), CR ID G00010530. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork skin (cooked) data as a secondary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bi Cuon (pork skin roll). The HPB-reported carbohydrate content is 0.0 g/100g, indicating negligible available carbohydrate in the dish as analysed. Because carbohydrate_g is confirmed at 0.0 g/100g by HPB, glycemic load per serving is 0.0. A nominal GI of 40 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate based on rice paper-wrapped Vietnamese rolls (closest analogous food in University of Sydney GI Database: spring rolls / rice paper rolls, GI ~40), acknowledging that the pork skin and protein content dominate this dish and the effective glycemic impact is negligible. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 0) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; potassium at 47 mg/100g provides negligible offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Bi Cuon due to its very high sodium content (1050 mg/100g), and should request reduced fish sauce in preparation and avoid additional dipping sauce to minimise sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g for the pork skin component; however, rice paper wrapper and roasted rice powder contribute refined high-GI carbohydrates in the assembled dish, and fish sauce-based dipping sauce may contain added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should be aware that while the pork skin component has negligible carbohydrate, the rice paper wrapper and roasted rice powder add refined carbohydrates that promote glucose absorption, and the dipping sauce should be avoided or requested without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), elevated cholesterol (89 mg/100g), saturated fat from pork skin, and collagen-derived advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Bi Cuon due to its combination of very high sodium, significant cholesterol, and saturated fat from pork skin, all of which contribute to arterial stiffness, elevated blood pressure, and atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork skin is high in saturated fat and collagen-derived AGEs; pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Bi Cuon as pork skin is among the most pro-inflammatory cuts due to its saturated fat, AGE content, and arachidonic acid load, compounded by very high sodium that exacerbates joint tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Skin Roll (Bi Cuon) is a Vietnamese dish popular in Singapore's hawker centres and Vietnamese food stalls. It consists of shredded boiled pork skin (bì) mixed with roasted rice powder (thính) and sometimes shredded pork, wrapped in a thin rice paper (bánh tráng) together with fresh herbs such as mint and coriander. The roll is typically served with a fish sauce-based dipping sauce (nước chấm) that may contain sugar, lime juice, garlic, and chilli. The texture is chewy from the pork skin, fragrant from the roasted rice powder, and fresh from the herbs. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), with very high sodium (1050 mg/100g) driven by fish sauce and seasoning. The HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g for the pork skin component, though the rice paper wrapper and roasted rice powder contribute refined carbohydrates in the assembled dish. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very high sodium content, and caution is warranted for those with cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes (due to rice paper and dipping sauce), and rheumatoid arthritis (due to pork skin's pro-inflammatory saturated fat and AGE content). It is not halal as it contains pork.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Pork Skin Roll (Bi Cuon), CR ID G00010530, with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork skin (cooked) as a secondary reference since HPB does not report this field for this entry.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo (G00010530); this reflects the pork skin and shredded pork component as analysed — the rice paper wrapper and roasted rice powder contribute carbohydrates in the assembled dish but the HPB composite value reports 0.0 g/100g. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB. These are confirmed zero values from the approved HPB source, not assumed. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this entry and not available in a directly matching approved source record. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported by HPB for this entry and no directly matching approved source provides complete micronutrient data for this composite dish. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.6 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for pork skin (cooked), as HPB does not report saturated fat for this record; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN source if available. GI assigned as analogous estimate (GI ~40, low category) based on rice paper-wrapped Vietnamese rolls from the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per serving is 0.0 given confirmed zero carbohydrate in the HPB-analysed component.
